items.title: Before You Migrate: Five Surprising Ingress-NGINX Behaviors You Need to Know
items.summary:
<p>As <a href="https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/11/11/ingress-nginx-retirement/">announced</a> November 2025, Kubernetes will retire Ingress-NGINX in March 2026.
Despite its widespread usage, Ingress-NGINX is full of surprising defaults and side effects that are probably present in your clus
items.reason: Important upcoming change: Ingress‑NGINX retirement affects Kubernetes deployments; no existing wiki page covers this migration guidance.

items.title: Docker Model Runner Brings vLLM to macOS with Apple Silicon
items.summary: vLLM has quickly become the go-to inference engine for developers who need high-throughput LLM serving. We brought vLLM to Docker Model Runner for NVIDIA GPUs on Linux, then extended it to Windows via WSL2. That changes today. Docker Model Runner now supports vllm-metal, a new backend that brings vL
items.reason: Introduces Docker Model Runner with vLLM on Apple Silicon – a notable development for AI/ML workloads on macOS that warrants its own documentation.

items.title: Open WebUI + Docker Model Runner: Self-Hosted Models, Zero Configuration
items.summary: We’re excited to share a seamless new integration between Docker Model Runner (DMR) and Open WebUI, bringing together two open source projects to make working with self-hosted models easier than ever. With this update, Open WebUI automatically detects and connects to Docker Model Runner running at l
items.reason: Covers integration of Open WebUI and Docker Model Runner, a useful reference for self‑hosted model serving; not yet documented.

items.title: From the Captain’s Chair: Kristiyan Velkov
items.summary: Docker Captains are leaders from the developer community that are both experts in their field and are passionate about sharing their Docker knowledge with others. “From the Captain’s Chair” is a blog series where we get a closer look at one Captain to learn more about them and their experiences. Tod
items.reason: Community interview content; low technical depth and already covered by general community pages, so no dedicated wiki needed.

items.title: Gordon (Beta): Docker’s AI Agent Just Got an Update
items.summary: AI agents are moving from demos to daily workflows. They write code, run commands, and complete multi-step tasks without constant hand-holding. But general-purpose agents don't know Docker. They don't understand your containers, your images, or your specific setup. Gordon does. Just run docker ai in
items.reason: Docker’s AI agent ‘Gordon’ update introduces new capabilities that impact Docker‑based AI workflows; requires a dedicated article.

items.title: Run OpenClaw Securely in Docker Sandboxes
items.summary: Docker Sandboxes is a new primitive in the Docker's ecosystem that allows you to run AI agents or any other workloads in isolated micro VMs. It provides strong isolation, convenient developer experience and a strong security boundary with a network proxy configurable to deny agents connecting to arb
items.reason: Docker Sandboxes for secure AI agent execution is a new security primitive; worth documenting for developers.

items.title: What’s new with GitHub Copilot coding agent
items.summary:
GitHub Copilot coding agent now includes a model picker, self-review, built-in security scanning, custom agents, and CLI handoff. Here's what's new and how to use it.

items.reason: New features of GitHub Copilot coding agent (model picker, self‑review, security scanning) merit a detailed wiki entry.

items.title: Multi-agent workflows often fail. Here’s how to engineer ones that don’t.
items.summary:
Most multi-agent workflow failures come down to missing structure, not model capability. Learn the three engineering patterns that make agent systems reliable.
The post Multi-agent workflows often fail. Here’s how to engineer ones that don’t. appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Practical patterns for reliable multi‑agent workflows are valuable for AI system designers; not yet covered.

items.title: How AI is reshaping developer choice (and Octoverse data proves it)
items.summary:
AI is rewiring developer preferences through convenience loops. Octoverse 2025 reveals how AI compatibility is becoming the new standard for technology choice.
The post How AI is reshaping developer choice (and Octoverse data proves it) appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Analysis of AI’s impact on developer tool choice, backed by Octoverse data, provides insight for strategic decisions and should be documented.

### 🐛 Fixes
* emit container status events after network reconnection (fixes #13524) by @MaheshThakur9152 in https://github.com/docker/compose/pull/13529
* Fix potential nil pointer dereference in container event monitoring by @Nepomuk5665 in https://github.com/docker/compose

items.title: oven-sh/bun bun-v1.3.10
items.summary:
To install Bun v1.3.10
```bash
curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
# or you can use npm
# npm install -g bun
```

Windows:
```bash
powershell -c "irm bun.sh/install.ps1|iex"
```

To upgrade to Bun v1.3.10:
```bash
bun upgrade
```

### [Read Bun v1.3.10's release notes on Bun's
items.reason: Bun v1.3.10 release notes provide installation and upgrade steps; useful for JavaScript runtime users.
